perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the provisions of Paragraph 1 above, the Parties may disclose the Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement to their employees and/or agents, but only for the purpose of supplying the Party with sufficient information to enable the Party to evaluate the potential value of establishing a business and/or contractual relationship with each other. The Recipient will inform each of its employees, agents and affiliates (collectively, "Representatives") who will receive Confidential Information of the obligations under this Agreement and agrees to take all commercially reasonable measures to restrain its Representatives from taking any action that would constitute a breach of the terms of this Agreement. In any event, the Recipient shall be responsible for any breach of the terms of this Agreement by any of its Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the provisions of Paragraph 1 above, the Parties may disclose the Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement to their employees and/or agents, but only for the purpose of supplying the Party with sufficient information to enable the Party to evaluate the potential value of establishing a business and/or contractual relationship with each other. The Recipient will inform each of its employees, agents and affiliates (collectively, "Associated Entities") who will receive Confidential Information of the obligations under this Agreement and agrees to take all commercially reasonable measures to restrain its Associated Entities from taking any action that would constitute a breach of the terms of this Agreement. In any event, the Recipient shall be responsible for any breach of the terms of this Agreement by any of its Associated Entities.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'Representatives' is changed to 'Associated Entities'. This creates an in-text contradiction because 'Representatives' is explicitly defined as 'employees, agents and affiliates' while 'Associated Entities' is not defined. Therefore, the scope of responsibility for breaches of the agreement is unclear. It becomes uncertain if the Recipient is only responsible for the breach commited only by those under Associated Entities or only by those under Representatives.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Notwithstanding the provisions of Paragraph 1 above, the Parties may disclose the Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement to their employees and/or agents, but only for the purpose of supplying the Party with sufficient information to enable the Party to evaluate the potential value of establishing a business and/or contractual relationship with each other. The Recipient will inform each of its employees, agents and affiliates (collectively, "Associated Entities") who will receive Confidential Information of the obligations under this Agreement and agrees to take all commercially reasonable measures to restrain its Associated Entities from taking any action that would constitute a breach of the terms of this Agreement. In any event, the Recipient shall be responsible for any breach of the terms of this Agreement by any of its Associated Entities.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Representatives' to 'Associated Entities' creates a contradiction. The original text explicitly defines 'Representatives' as 'employees, agents and affiliates', clarifying the scope of the Recipient's responsibility for breaches. The changed text uses 'Associated Entities' without providing a definition. The contradicted text is the same as the changed text. This change introduces ambiguity regarding the scope of the Recipient's responsibility, making the agreement unclear and potentially unenforceable, since now it's not clear if the Recipient is only responsible for the breach committed by those under Associated Entities or only by those under Representatives.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties hereto agree that information shall not be deemed Confidential Information, and Recipient shall have no obligation with respect to any such information which: (i) was generally known to the public prior to the disclosure under this Agreement; 1 <PAGE> (ii) is already known to Recipient prior to October 27, 1999, as evidenced by the written electronically stored records of the Party dated prior to October 27, 1999; or (iii) is or becomes publicly known through no wrongful act of Recipient or any person to whom the Recipient discloses such information; or (iv) is received by a third party without breach of this Agreement or any other obligation to maintain the confidentiality of such information; (v) is independently developed by Recipient; or (vi) is approved for release by written authorization of Owner; or (vii) is disclosed pursuant to the lawful requirement or request of a governmental agency, or disclosure is required by operation of law.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties hereto agree that information shall not be deemed Confidential Data, and Recipient shall have no obligation with respect to any such information which: (i) was generally known to the public prior to the disclosure under this Agreement; 1 <PAGE> (ii) is already known to Recipient prior to October 27, 1999, as evidenced by the written electronically stored records of the Party dated prior to October 27, 1999; or (iii) is or becomes publicly known through no wrongful act of Recipient or any person to whom the Recipient discloses such information; or (iv) is received by a third party without breach of this Agreement or any other obligation to maintain the confidentiality of such information; (v) is independently developed by Recipient; or (vi) is approved for release by written authorization of Owner; or (vii) is disclosed pursuant to the lawful requirement or request of a governmental agency, or disclosure is required by operation of law.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Confidential Data' creates ambiguity. The agreement starts by defining 'Confidential Information' and uses that term throughout. Switching to 'Confidential Data' in Section 3 introduces a contradiction; it becomes unclear if the exceptions listed apply to the originally defined 'Confidential Information', or a new, undefined term 'Confidential Data', or both.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Parties hereto agree that information shall not be deemed Confidential Data, and Recipient shall have no obligation with respect to any such information which: (i) was generally known to the public prior to the disclosure under this Agreement; 1 <PAGE> (ii) is already known to Recipient prior to October 27, 1999, as evidenced by the written electronically stored records of the Party dated prior to October 27, 1999; or (iii) is or becomes publicly known through no wrongful act of Recipient or any person to whom the Recipient discloses such information; or (iv) is received by a third party without breach of this Agreement or any other obligation to maintain the confidentiality of such information; (v) is independently developed by Recipient; or (vi) is approved for release by written authorization of Owner; or (vii) is disclosed pursuant to the lawful requirement or request of a governmental agency, or disclosure is required by operation of law.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Confidential Information' to 'Confidential Data' creates a contradiction. The original agreement consistently uses 'Confidential Information,' and switching to 'Confidential Data' introduces an undefined term, potentially invalidating or creating ambiguity around the exceptions to confidentiality listed in the contradicted text, which still uses the new term 'Confidential Data.' This aligns with the Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction definition because the contract now uses two terms, 'Confidential Information' and 'Confidential Data,' without defining them, leading to conflicting interpretations regarding the applicability of the exceptions listed.

perturbation.original_text: All written data delivered by Owner to the Recipient pursuant to this Agreement shall be and remain the property of Owner, and all such written data, and all copies thereof, shall be promptly returned to Owner upon written request, or destroyed at Owner's option.
perturbation.changed_text: All tangible material delivered by Owner to the Recipient pursuant to this Agreement shall be and remain the property of Owner, and all such written data, and all copies thereof, shall be promptly returned to Owner upon written request, or destroyed at Owner's option.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'written data' to 'tangible material' creates a contradiction. The phrase 'all such written data, and all copies thereof' is maintained in the clause. If the phrase was modified to follow the change, then there wouldn't be a contradiction. The contradiction arises because the phrase specifies 'written data' which is narrower than 'tangible material', thus introducing uncertainty about what must be returned or destroyed. A tangible material can also be a USB key, is that also implied by the clause?
perturbation.contradicted_text: All tangible material delivered by Owner to the Recipient pursuant to this Agreement shall be and remain the property of Owner, and all such written data, and all copies thereof, shall be promptly returned to Owner upon written request, or destroyed at Owner's option.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'written data' to 'tangible material' introduces a contradiction. While the original text specified 'written data' for return/destruction, the modified text broadens the scope to 'tangible material' but retains the phrase 'all such written data, and all copies thereof'. This creates an inconsistency as the clause now refers to tangible material in general but then focuses specifically on written data, leading to uncertainty about which tangible materials are subject to the return/destruction requirement. Because the Changed Text and Contradicted Text are the same, and the Explanation correctly describes how the change leads to a contradiction, the answer is YES.
